## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Project Selection (KTU Approved Framework)

Every project topic — whether T-Level, D-Level, or R-Level — **must satisfy the three-pillar test** before being approved by the faculty guide:

1. **Pillar P1 — Technical Feasibility**
   The topic must be implementable using concepts covered in **Modules 1 to 4** of PBEET404. Modules 1–3 cover number systems, Boolean algebra, combinational circuits (adders, multiplexers, decoders, encoders), and sequential circuits (flip-flops, registers, counters). Module 4 covers **memory devices, programmable logic, and project-level integration**.

2. **Pillar P2 — Resource Availability**
   The required ICs (e.g., 7400-series TTL, 4000-series CMOS), development boards (Arduino Uno, ESP32, FPGA Spartan-6), sensors, and software licenses (Xilinx Vivado, ModelSim, Logisim) must be available **in-house** in the college lab or **open-source**.

3. **Pillar P3 — Outcome Mapping (OBE Compliance)**
   The project **MUST** explicitly state which **Course Outcomes (COs)** and **Program Outcomes (POs)** it addresses. A sample mapping is shown in § 2.4 below.

### 2.2 High-Yield Master List of 40+ Suggested Project Topics (Module-Aligned)

The following list is **directly drawn from the KTU-recommended project bank** for the **PBEET404 – Digital Electronics** course. Each topic is tagged with its primary module dependency, difficulty level, and approximate bill of materials (BOM).

#### 2.2.1 T-Level Projects (Foundational, < ₹500, 1–2 weeks)

These projects target students who are **first-time circuit builders** and rely exclusively on **discrete TTL/CMOS ICs** from the 7400 and 4000 series.

- **T-01:** Binary to Gray code and Gray to Binary code converter using **XOR gates (7486)**.
- **T-02:** BCD to 7-segment display decoder driver using **7447 / 7448 ICs** with common-anode displays.
- **T-03:** 4-bit parallel adder/subtractor using **7483 (4-bit binary full adder)** with mode control.
- **T-04:** 8:1 Multiplexer and 1:8 Demultiplexer demonstration board using **74151 / 74154**.
- **T-05:** SR, JK, D, and T flip-flop verification kit using **7400 (NAND) and 7476 (JK)** on a single breadboard.
- **T-06:** 4-bit synchronous up/down counter with **7476 JK flip-flops** and 7447 display decoder.
- **T-07:** Digital dice circuit using **7490 decade counter** and 7-segment display (rolling effect via clock).
- **T-08:** 2-bit / 4-bit magnitude comparator implementation using **7485 IC**.
- **T-09:** 4-bit shift register (SISO, SIPO, PISO, PIPO) demonstration using **7495 IC**.
- **T-10:** Even and odd parity generator/checker using **74180 IC**.

#### 2.2.2 D-Level Projects (Intermediate, ₹500 – ₹2,000, 3–4 weeks)

These projects require **microcontroller integration (Arduino/ESP32)** or **FPGA implementation (Xilinx Vivado + Spartan-6)** and demonstrate **system-level design thinking**.

- **D-01:** **Digital Stopwatch with Lap Function** — using Arduino UNO, 16×2 LCD, and 4 push buttons (Start, Stop, Reset, Lap). Time base: Timer1 in CTC mode at $1\,\text{Hz}$.
- **D-02:** **Traffic Light Controller with Emergency Override** — using 8051 / Arduino, with sensor input for emergency vehicle detection.
- **D-03:** **8-bit ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) on FPGA** — implementing ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, NOT, INC, DEC operations; 4:1 MUX for operation selection; status flags (Zero, Carry, Sign, Parity).
- **D-04:** **Digital Thermometer with LCD Display** — Arduino UNO + LM35 / DS18B20 sensor + 16×2 LCD; Celsius/Fahrenheit toggle.
- **D-05:** **Voting Machine (4-candidate)** — using 7474 D flip-flops for latching and 7447 for 7-segment display.
- **D-06:** **Digital Clock with RTC (DS1307/DS3231)** — Arduino-based, with hour-minute-second display, 12/24 hour mode.
- **D-07:** **Frequency Divider / Square Wave Generator** — using 7490 decade counter in divide-by-N configuration for sub-Hz frequencies.
- **D-08:** **BCD Adder** — using two 7483 ICs cascaded with proper carry chain and BCD correction (add 6 if sum > 9).
- **D-09:** **16-key Hex Keypad Encoder** — using 74147 priority encoder and 7-segment display.
- **D-10:** **Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) using LFSR** — 8-bit / 16-bit Linear Feedback Shift Register implemented on FPGA or with 7495.
- **D-11:** **Digital Water Level Indicator** — using 7-segment display with float sensor inputs through comparators (LM324).
- **D-12:** **Password-Based Door Lock System** — Arduino + 4×4 matrix keypad + 16×2 LCD + solenoid lock; password stored in EEPROM.
- **D-13:** **LED Matrix Scrolling Display (8×8)** — Arduino + MAX7219 driver, displaying scrolling text via Charlieplexing.
- **D-14:** **Sequence Detector (Mealy/Moore machine)** — detecting "1011" or "1100" pattern using D flip-flops on FPGA or discrete 7474.
- **D-15:** **Calculator (4-bit) using FPGA** — 4×4 keypad input, 7-segment display output, operations +, –, ×.

#### 2.2.3 R-Level Projects (Research-Oriented, ₹2,000 – ₹5,000, 5–6 weeks)

These projects require **advanced integration**, **PCB design**, or **IoT/embedded systems** knowledge. They are recommended for high-performing students targeting GATE, IES, or M.Tech admissions.

- **R-01:** **FPGA-based UART Communication Module** — implementing asynchronous serial transmitter/receiver on Spartan-6 using VHDL/Verilog, with baud-rate generator (programmable divisor).
- **R-02:** **I2C Protocol Master-Slave Communication between two microcontrollers** — Arduino as master, ESP32 as slave; sensor data exchange.
- **R-03:** **SPI-Based DAC Interface (MCP4921)** — generating arbitrary waveforms (sine, triangular, sawtooth) from a digital look-up table stored in PROGMEM.
- **R-04:** **Digital Notice Board using IoT (ESP32 + WiFi + LED Matrix)** — receiving messages from a web server (Blynk/ThingSpeak) and displaying on 8×32 P10 LED matrix.
- **R-05:** **FPGA Implementation of FIR Filter** — 4-tap / 8-tap Finite Impulse Response filter for low-pass digital signal processing, verified via ModelSim and synthesized on Spartan-6.
- **R-06:** **Vending Machine Controller using FSM** — Mealy machine with 5 states (Idle, Coin-Insert, Selection, Dispense, Change-Return), implemented on FPGA.
- **R-07:** **Elevator Controller with Floor Display** — 4-floor elevator using priority encoder + up/down counter + 7-segment display; door open/close timing.
- **R-08:** **Automatic Railway Gate Controller** — using IR sensors, servo motor, 7-segment countdown timer, and Arduino.
- **R-09:** **Home Automation via Bluetooth (HC-05) and Arduino** — 4-channel relay control from Android app (MIT App Inventor), with status feedback.
- **R-10:** **PWM Generation using 555 Timer + Digital Control** — variable duty cycle PWM for motor speed control, with digital potentiometer (MCP4131) setting duty cycle via SPI.
- **R-11:** **FPGA-Based VGA Display Driver** — generating 640×480 @ 60 Hz VGA signal displaying test patterns (color bars, checkerboard) on a monitor.
- **R-12:** **Cryptographic Hash Function (SHA-1 simplified) Implementation on FPGA** — for advanced students; requires understanding of bitwise operations and state machines.
- **R-13:** **Smart Energy Meter with Digital Display** — using current sensor (ACS712), Arduino, and LCD; displaying kWh consumed, voltage, current, power factor.
- **R-14:** **4-bit RISC Processor on FPGA** — custom instruction set (LDA, STA, ADD, SUB, JMP, JZ), 16-byte memory, accumulator-based architecture.
- **R-15:** **Multi-Mode Digital Voltmeter** — Arduino + analog multiplexer (CD4051) selecting from 4 voltage divider ranges, auto-ranging, ±0.1 V accuracy.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Project Viability Cheat Sheet

The following table is a **rapid-reference guide** for evaluating the feasibility, cost, and learning value of any digital electronics project before submission to the faculty guide.

| Decision Parameter | Formula / Threshold | Interpretation |
|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Component Count (CC)** | $CC = \sum (\text{IC count} + \text{passive components})$ | If $CC > 80$, switch to microcontroller/FPGA implementation. |
| **Estimated Cost (EC)** | $EC = \sum (\text{unit price} \times \text{quantity})$ | If $EC > \text{₹ 5,000}$, faculty approval with budget justification required. |
| **Development Time (DT)** | $DT = T_{\text{design}} + T_{\text{build}} + T_{\text{test}}$ | If $DT > 6$ weeks, scope must be reduced. |
| **Power Budget (PB)** | $PB = V_{CC} \times I_{total}$ (in $\text{mW}$) | If $PB > 5\,\text{W}$, add heatsink or use switching regulator. |
| **Logic Gate Equivalent (LGE)** | $LGE = 4 \times \text{flip-flops} + 1 \times \text{gates}$ | Used to estimate **FPGA LUT utilization** in advance. |
| **Clock Frequency Limit** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{pd,FF} + T_{pd,combinational} + T_{setup}}$ | Determines maximum operating speed. |
| **Propagation Delay Budget** | $T_{pd,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{pd,i}$ | Cumulative delay through $n$ cascaded gates. |
| **Fan-out (TTL)** | $FO = \left\lfloor \dfrac{I_{OL}}{I_{IL}} \right\rfloor$ | Number of standard TTL loads a gate can drive. |
| **Noise Margin** | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}, \quad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Higher $NM$ → more noise-tolerant design. |
| **CO Coverage Score** | $\text{Score} = \dfrac{\text{Number of COs addressed}}{5}$ | Target: $\text{Score} \geq 0.6$ (i.e., at least 3 of 5 COs). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 OBE Reminder:** When submitting your project proposal, you **MUST** fill the **Course Outcome Mapping Table** (see § 2.4) and attach it on Page 2 of your project report. This is **not optional** — reports without CO mapping are **auto-penalized 5 marks** by the external examiner.

### 2.4 Sample OBE Mapping Table (for Project D-03: FPGA-based 8-bit ALU)

| Project Activity | Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Program Outcome (PO) |
|------------------|---------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Design 4:1 MUX for operation selection | **CO2** – Design combinational circuits | L4 (Analyze) | PO1, PO3 |
| Implement ADD/SUB using ripple carry | **CO2** – Combinational logic | L4 (Analyze) | PO1, PO3 |
| Generate status flags (Zero, Carry) | **CO3** – Sequential design | L5 (Evaluate) | PO1, PO3 |
| Simulate using ModelSim / Vivado | **CO4** – Use EDA tools | L3 (Apply) | PO5 |
| Document HDL code and testbench | **CO5** – Communication | L3 (Apply) | PO10 |
| Final demo on FPGA board | **CO4** – Hardware verification | L5 (Evaluate) | PO5, PO12 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

A digital electronics project is **not an academic exercise in isolation** — every topic above maps to a **real industry vertical**:

- **Automotive:** R-08 (Railway Gate), D-02 (Traffic Controller) → embedded systems in **Bosch, Continental, Wipro Automotive**.
- **IoT / Smart Home:** R-04 (LED Notice Board), R-09 (Bluetooth Home Automation) → product lines at **Tata Elxsi, Honeywell, Schneider Electric**.
- **VLSI / Chip Design:** R-01 (UART), R-05 (FIR Filter), R-11 (VGA), R-14 (RISC) → career pathway at **Intel, AMD, Qualcomm, Texas Instruments, Synopsys, Cadence**.
- **Industrial Automation:** D-07 (Vending Machine), R-06 (FSM Vending) → PLC/DCS roles at **Siemens, ABB, Mitsubishi Electric**.
- **Consumer Electronics:** D-01 (Stopwatch), D-06 (Digital Clock) → firmware roles at **Sony, Samsung, LG, boAt**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The KTU Project Execution Lifecycle (7-Phase Derivation)

Unlike a plug-and-play formula, the **KTU 2024 mini-project evaluation rubric** is derived from a **7-phase execution model**. Each phase has **explicit deliverable artifacts** that the student must produce. The derivation below is the canonical workflow that the KTU Board Examiner expects to see in a high-scoring project report.

#### Phase 1 — Problem Definition & CO Mapping

**Deliverable:** A **2-page proposal** with title, problem statement, objectives, scope, and CO/PO mapping.

**Step-by-step:**

1. Select a topic from the § 2.2 list (or a novel equivalent, subject to guide approval).
2. Write the **problem statement** in **one sentence** using the formula: *"To design and implement a [system] that performs [function] using [technology], addressing [real-world problem]."*
3. List **3–5 measurable objectives** beginning with action verbs: *design, simulate, fabricate, test, demonstrate*.
4. Fill the **OBE mapping table** (§ 2.4).
5. Submit to faculty guide for **Phase-1 sign-off**.

#### Phase 2 — Literature Survey & Component Selection

**Deliverable:** A **2–3 page literature review** citing **at least 4 references** (textbooks, IEEE papers, datasheets). No Wikipedia, no random blogs.

**Step-by-step:**

1. Search **IEEE Xplore**, **Google Scholar**, and **Texas Instruments / Xilinx datasheets** for the chosen topic.
2. Read **at least 2 prior implementations** and note their **strengths and limitations**.
3. Finalize the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** using the formula:

$$ \text{EC}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times Q_i) $$

where $P_i$ is the unit price of the $i$-th component and $Q_i$ is the quantity. Submit BOM with vendor links (e.g., Robu.in, Amazon.in, electronicscomp.com).

#### Phase 3 — Circuit Design & Schematic Capture

**Deliverable:** A **schematic diagram** drawn in **KiCad / EasyEDA / Fritzing** or hand-drawn neatly (if KTU allows). **Pin numbers, IC numbers, and net labels** must be visible.

**Step-by-step:**

1. Translate the problem into a **block diagram** (e.g., for Digital Stopwatch: Input → Clock Generator → Counter → Decoder → Display).
2. Select specific **IC families** (TTL: 74xx, CMOS: 40xx, or microcontroller).
3. Draw the **complete schematic** with:
   - Power supply rails ($V_{CC} = +5\,\text{V}$, $GND = 0\,\text{V}$).
   - Decoupling capacitors ($0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ ceramic) on **every IC's $V_{CC}$ pin** — this is a **mandatory KTU rule** and a **common deduction point**.
   - Pull-up / pull-down resistors on unused inputs.
4. **Double-check the design** using the **propagation delay budget equation**:

$$ T_{pd,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{pd,i} \leq T_{clock} - T_{setup} $$

where $T_{clock}$ is the clock period and $T_{setup}$ is the flip-flop setup time. If $T_{pd,total}$ exceeds this bound, **redesign with faster logic family** (e.g., 74HC instead of 74LS, or 74F series).

#### Phase 4 — Simulation (Pre-Hardware Verification)

**Deliverable:** **Simulation screenshots / waveforms** with annotated inputs and outputs.

**Step-by-step:**

1. For **discrete IC projects:** Use **TinkerCAD**, **Proteus**, or **Logisim Evolution** to simulate before buying components.
2. For **FPGA projects:** Use **Xilinx Vivado** (free WebPack edition) or **Intel Quartus Prime Lite**.
3. For **microcontroller projects:** Use **TinkerCAD Arduino Simulator** or **Wokwi** for initial code testing.
4. **Verify the truth table** matches the **expected Boolean expressions** from the design phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Phase 4):** A common 5-mark deduction occurs when students **skip simulation** and jump directly to hardware. KTU 2024 specifically requires **simulation evidence** as part of the 20 internal marks. **Always include simulation screenshots** in the report's Appendix B.

#### Phase 5 — Hardware Implementation

**Deliverable:** A **working circuit** on breadboard, perfboard, or custom PCB.

**Step-by-step:**

1. Place ICs on breadboard with **pin-1 indicator always on the left** (universal convention).
2. Connect **$V_{CC}$ (pin 14 for 7400-series DIP-14, pin 16 for DIP-16) and GND (pin 7 / pin 8 respectively)** to every IC.
3. Add **$0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ decoupling capacitor** between $V_{CC}$ and GND of every IC, as close to the IC body as possible.
4. Use **color-coded wires**: red = $V_{CC}$, black = GND, others for signals.
5. Power up using a **current-limited bench power supply** (set to 100 mA limit first, increase if needed).
6. Test each **functional block independently** before integrating.

#### Phase 6 — Testing, Debugging & Validation

**Deliverable:** A **test results table** comparing **expected vs. observed outputs** for at least **10 test cases**.

**Step-by-step:**

1. Prepare a **test case matrix** with columns: *Test Case ID, Input Condition, Expected Output, Observed Output, Pass/Fail, Remarks*.
2. Test **boundary conditions** (all 0s, all 1s, transitions, race conditions).
3. For sequential circuits, test for **setup time ($t_s$) and hold time ($t_h$) violations** using:

$$ t_s \leq T_{clock} - T_{pd,combinational,max} - T_{pd,FF,clk-to-Q} $$

$$ t_h \leq T_{pd,combinational,min} + T_{pd,FF,clk-to-Q,min} $$

4. Document any **bugs found and fixed** — examiners **reward** debug narratives.

#### Phase 7 — Report Writing & Presentation

**Deliverable:** A **20–30 page project report** in **KTU-mandated format** + a **10-minute presentation**.

**Step-by-step KTU Report Structure:**

1. **Title Page** — Project title, student names, roll numbers, guide name, department, academic year.
2. **Certificate Page** — Signed by guide, HOD, and principal.
3. **Acknowledgement** (1 page max).
4. **Abstract** (200–250 words) — summarising the problem, method, and result.
5. **Table of Contents** + **List of Figures** + **List of Tables**.
6. **Chapter 1 – Introduction** (3–4 pages): problem statement, objectives, scope, CO/PO mapping.
7. **Chapter 2 – Literature Review** (2–3 pages).
8. **Chapter 3 – System Design** (4–5 pages): block diagram, schematic, truth tables, K-maps, Boolean expressions.
9. **Chapter 4 – Implementation** (3–4 pages): hardware photos, simulation screenshots, code snippets (if applicable).
10. **Chapter 5 – Testing & Results** (3–4 pages): test cases, expected vs. observed, performance analysis.
11. **Chapter 6 – Conclusion & Future Scope** (1–2 pages).
12. **References** — IEEE format, minimum 6 references.
13. **Appendices** — Datasheets, full code listing, raw test data.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Project Complexity vs. Learning Outcome

The matrix below is derived by correlating **Bloom's cognitive level** with **circuit complexity** (measured in number of ICs / HDL lines). Use this as a **self-assessment rubric** before submitting your project.

| Bloom's Level | CO Reference | Circuit Complexity (IC count) | HDL Lines (FPGA) | Project Examples |
|---------------|--------------|-------------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| L3 – Apply | CO1, CO2 | 1 – 3 ICs | $\leq 50$ | T-01, T-02, T-05 |
| L4 – Analyze | CO2, CO3 | 4 – 8 ICs | 50 – 200 | D-03, D-08, D-10, D-14 |
| L5 – Evaluate | CO3, CO4 | 9 – 15 ICs **or** 1 FPGA + peripherals | 200 – 500 | R-01, R-05, R-11, R-14 |
| L6 – Create | CO4, CO5 | Novel architecture | $\geq 500$ | R-12 (SHA), R-14 (RISC) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Recommendation:** Aim for a project that sits **firmly in L4 or L5** — these are the "sweet spot" difficulty levels that earn **full marks (45–50/50)** without being so ambitious that you fail to complete it in 6 weeks.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Selecting the Right Project

Let us walk through a **realistic decision-making process** for a 2-member student team, "Student A" (strong in C programming) and "Student B" (strong in circuit soldering), at Sree Buddha College of Engineering, Pathanamthitta.

**Step 1 — Skill Inventory:**

$$\text{Skills} = \{\text{Programming}, \text{Hardware}, \text{EDA Tools}, \text{Documentation}\}$$

Student A: $\text{Programming} = 8/10, \text{Hardware} = 4/10, \text{EDA Tools} = 6/10$.
Student B: $\text{Programming} = 5/10, \text{Hardware} = 8/10, \text{EDA Tools} = 4/10$.

**Step 2 — Combined Team Strength:**

$$\text{Team Strength} = (8+5, \; 4+8, \; 6+4, \; 5+5) = (13, 12, 10, 10)$$

Normalized to a 10-point scale: $(6.5, 6.0, 5.0, 5.0)$.

**Step 3 — Project Selection Filter:**

- **Filter A — Hardware dependency:** Team hardware score is $6.0$ → projects with $\text{Hardware dependency} \leq 6$ are preferred.
- **Filter B — Programming dependency:** Team programming score is $6.5$ → projects with $\text{Programming} \leq 7$ are preferred.

**Step 4 — Cross-Reference with Project Bank:**

Looking at the § 2.2.2 (D-Level) list, **D-12 (Password-Based Door Lock System)** scores:
- Hardware dependency: $5/10$ ✓ (one solenoid, one LCD, one keypad, one Arduino).
- Programming dependency: $7/10$ ✓ (Arduino sketch with EEPROM, debouncing, LCD I2C).
- EDA tools: $4/10$ (TinkerCAD simulation).
- Documentation: $6/10$ (well-documented Arduino libraries).

**All four filters pass — D-12 is selected.**

**Step 5 — CO Mapping (D-12):**

| Activity | CO | PO |
|----------|----|----|
| Keypad scanning algorithm | CO1 | PO1 |
| Solenoid driver circuit design (transistor + flyback diode) | CO2 | PO1, PO3 |
| EEPROM password storage | CO3 | PO3, PO5 |
| LCD I2C interfacing | CO4 | PO5 |
| Final demo + report | CO5 | PO10, PO12 |

$$\text{CO Coverage Score} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0 \;\; \text{(Excellent — exceeds KTU threshold of 0.6)}$$

This **derivation method** should be used by every student team before finalizing the project topic.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — KTU Project Execution Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart represents the **canonical 7-phase KTU project execution lifecycle**, with **decision diamonds** at the critical sign-off points.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Topic Selection"] --> B["Phase 1: Problem Definition & CO Mapping"]
    B --> C{"Guide Approval Phase 1?"}
    C -- "No" --> B
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Phase 2: Literature Survey & BOM"]
    D --> E{"Budget within Rs 5000?"}
    E -- "No" --> F["Scope Reduction or Grant Application"]
    F --> D
    E -- "Yes" --> G["Phase 3: Circuit Design & Schematic"]
    G --> H["Phase 4: Simulation Verification"]
    H --> I{"Simulation Pass?"}
    I -- "No" --> G
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Phase 5: Hardware Implementation"]
    J --> K["Phase 6: Testing & Validation"]
    K --> L{"All Test Cases Pass?"}
    L -- "No" --> M["Debug & Iterate"]
    M --> K
    L -- "Yes" --> N["Phase 7: Report Writing & Presentation"]
    N --> O["Internal Evaluation: 20 marks"]
    O --> P["External Viva-Voce: 30 marks"]
    P --> Q["End: Final Score out of 50"]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- **Rectangular nodes (A, B, D, G, H, J, K, N, O, P, Q)** represent **action phases** where the student actively works.
- **Diamond nodes (C, E, I, L)** represent **decision gates** that act as **quality checkpoints** — failing any one of them sends the project back to the previous phase.
- The **debug loop (M → K)** is explicitly modeled because **real engineering projects iterate 2–4 times** between testing and redesign.

```mermaid
    D3["D-03: 8-bit ALU on FPGA"]
    D14["D-14: Sequence Detector"]
    R1["R-01: UART on FPGA"]
    R5["R-05: FIR Filter on FPGA"]
    CO1 --> T1
    CO1 --> T2
    CO2 --> D3
    CO2 --> T2
    CO3 --> D14
    CO3 --> R1
    CO4 --> D3
    CO4 --> R1
    CO4 --> R5
    CO5 --> D3
    CO5 --> R5
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**List any six factors to be considered while selecting a mini-project topic in Digital Electronics.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each = 3 marks):**

1. **Technical feasibility** — implementable using concepts from PBEET404 Modules 1–4.
2. **Resource availability** — components / ICs / development boards must be accessible in the college lab or via online vendors.
3. **Cost** — total project expenditure should be within the KTU-mandated student budget (typically ≤ ₹5,000).
4. **Time duration** — completion possible within the 4–6 week mini-project window.
5. **Learning outcome mapping** — explicit coverage of at least 3 of the 5 Course Outcomes (COs).
6. **Demonstrability** — final working model should be presentable in a 10-minute viva-voce.

**Examiner's tip:** Avoid vague answers like *"interesting topic"*. Always anchor every factor to a **measurable parameter** (cost, time, CO count).

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Differentiate between T-Level, D-Level, and R-Level project categories as classified in the PBEET404 mini-project rubric.**

**Model Answer (3 × 1 mark = 3 marks):**

| Aspect | T-Level (Tinker) | D-Level (Design) | R-Level (Research) |
|--------|------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Bloom's Level** | L3 – Apply | L4 – Analyze | L5 – Evaluate / L6 – Create |
| **Component Count** | 1–3 ICs | 4–8 ICs or 1 microcontroller | FPGA or full system |
| **Cost** | < ₹500 | ₹500 – ₹2,000 | ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 |
| **Team Size** | 1 student | 2 students | 2–3 students |
| **Duration** | 1–2 weeks | 3–4 weeks | 5–6 weeks |
| **Example** | Binary–Gray converter (T-01) | 8-bit ALU on FPGA (D-03) | 4-bit RISC processor (R-14) |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module questions carry **14 marks** with an **internal choice** between two sub-options. The marks are typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Dec 2024]`

**(a)** *Your team of two students must select a Digital Electronics mini-project that maps to at least three Course Outcomes of PBEET404. Describe the **CO/PO mapping process** and justify the selection of the project **"D-12: Password-Based Door Lock System"** by listing the activities, the COs addressed, and the corresponding Bloom's levels. **\[7 marks\]***

**(b)** *Draw a **detailed block diagram** of the project D-12 showing all sub-modules (Keypad Interface, Controller, Display, Lock Actuator, Power Supply). For each sub-module, identify the **specific ICs / components** you would use, and justify your choices with at least one **technical reason** per module. **\[7 marks\]***

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — CO/PO Mapping Process (3 marks):**

The CO/PO mapping process involves four sub-steps:

1. **Identify the project activities** — break the project into 5–8 distinct tasks (e.g., keypad scanning, EEPROM write, solenoid driving, LCD display, debugging).
2. **Map each activity to a Course Outcome** — refer to the official PBEET404 CO statements (CO1: number systems / Boolean algebra, CO2: combinational circuits, CO3: sequential circuits, CO4: EDA tools / HDL, CO5: documentation / communication).
3. **Assign Bloom's level** — classify the cognitive demand of each activity (L1–L6).
4. **Map to Program Outcomes** — link to PO1, PO3, PO5, PO10, PO12 as applicable.

**Step 2 — Mapping Table for D-12 (4 marks):**

| # | Project Activity | CO | Bloom's Level | PO | Justification |
|---|------------------|-----|---------------|-----|---------------|
| 1 | Keypad matrix scanning algorithm (debounce + 4×4 decode) | **CO1** | L3 – Apply | PO1 | Uses binary-to-decimal conversion logic. |
| 2 | Design of transistor-driver circuit for solenoid (with flyback diode) | **CO2** | L4 – Analyze | PO1, PO3 | Combinational interface design. |
| 3 | Password storage and retrieval using ATmega328P internal EEPROM | **CO3** | L4 – Analyze | PO3, PO5 | Sequential write/read operations. |
| 4 | LCD I2C interfacing and real-time feedback | **CO4** | L3 – Apply | PO5 | EDA tool usage via Arduino IDE. |
| 5 | Final demonstration and 25-page report writing | **CO5** | L5 – Evaluate | PO10, PO12 | Communication of engineering solution. |

**CO Coverage Score:**

$$\text{Score} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0 \;\; \text{(Excellent — well above KTU threshold of 0.6)}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (description, 3 marks):**

The system consists of **five interconnected sub-modules**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["4x4 Matrix Keypad"] -- "8 lines (4 rows, 4 cols)" --> B["Arduino UNO Controller ATmega328P"]
    B -- "I2C SDA, SCL" --> C["16x2 LCD Display with PCF8574"]
    B -- "Digital pin D9 with MOSFET driver" --> D["Solenoid Lock Actuator"]
    B -- "5V regulated" --> E["Power Supply Unit: 12V adapter, LM7805 regulator, 1000uF filter"]
    B -- "Internal EEPROM" --> F["Password Storage: 4-byte address 0x00-0x03"]
```

**Sub-module Component Justification (4 marks):**

| Sub-module | Specific Component | Technical Justification |
|------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Keypad Input | 4×4 matrix membrane keypad | Provides 16 keys (0–9, A–D) using only 8 GPIO pins; debounce via software `$t_{debounce} = 20\,\text{ms}$`. |
| Controller | Arduino UNO (ATmega328P) | 14 digital I/O, 6 analog, $16\,\text{MHz}$ clock, built-in EEPROM — sufficient for keypad + LCD + lock. |
| Display | 16×2 LCD with I2C backpack (PCF8574, address $\text{0x27}$) | Uses only 2 pins (SDA, SCL) instead of 6 parallel pins, freeing GPIOs for lock and status LEDs. |
| Lock Actuator | 12V solenoid + IRF540N N-MOSFET + 1N4007 flyback diode | MOSFET switches $I_{solenoid} \leq 500\,\text{mA}$ safely; flyback diode protects against $V_{spike} = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ back-EMF. |
| Power Supply | 12V/1A adapter → LM7805 → $1000\,\mu\text{F}$ + $0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ filter | LM7805 provides regulated $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V} \pm 5\%$ with $I_{max} = 1\,\text{A}$, adequate for Arduino + LCD + keypad. |

**Operating Logic (1 mark):**

- On power-up, Arduino reads password from **EEPROM address 0x00–0x03** and displays **"Enter Password"** on LCD.
- User enters 4 digits via keypad; Arduino compares to stored value **digit-by-digit**.
- If match: drives MOSFET HIGH for 3 seconds → solenoid unlocks; LCD shows **"Access Granted"**; LED green.
- If no match in 3 attempts: LCD shows **"Access Denied"**; LED red; 30-second lockout.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Dec 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the **7-phase project execution lifecycle** prescribed by the KTU 2024 scheme for the PBEET404 mini-project. For each phase, list the **deliverable artifact** and the **typical time allocation**. **\[7 marks\]***

**(b)** *For the project **"R-05: FPGA Implementation of FIR Filter"**, identify the **specific HDL constructs** (VHDL or Verilog) required for: (i) a 4-tap FIR filter, (ii) a coefficient memory using ROM, and (iii) a testbench for ModelSim simulation. Provide a **structural architecture diagram** showing data flow from input → multipliers → adder tree → output register. **\[7 marks\]***

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**7-Phase Lifecycle (1 mark per phase, 7 total):**

| Phase | Activity | Deliverable Artifact | Time Allocation |
|-------|----------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Phase 1** | Problem Definition & CO Mapping | 2-page proposal + CO/PO table | 1 day |
| **Phase 2** | Literature Survey & BOM | 2–3 page literature review + BOM spreadsheet | 2–3 days |
| **Phase 3** | Circuit Design & Schematic | Schematic diagram + K-maps + Boolean expressions | 3–4 days |
| **Phase 4** | Simulation | Simulation screenshots / waveforms (.vcd or .do file) | 2–3 days |
| **Phase 5** | Hardware Implementation | Working circuit on breadboard / PCB | 1–2 weeks |
| **Phase 6** | Testing & Validation | Test case matrix with expected vs. observed | 3–4 days |
| **Phase 7** | Report Writing & Presentation | 20–30 page report + 10-minute PPT | 3–4 days |

**Total: ~5–6 weeks** (consistent with KTU's mini-project window).

**Critical Quality Checkpoints (built into the model):**

- **Phase 1:** Guide sign-off.
- **Phase 4:** Simulation pass before buying hardware.
- **Phase 6:** All test cases pass before report finalization.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**FIR Filter Theory Recap (1 mark):**

A 4-tap FIR filter computes the discrete-time convolution:

$$ y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{3} h[k] \cdot x[n-k] = h[0] \cdot x[n] + h[1] \cdot x[n-1] + h[2] \cdot x[n-2] + h[3] \cdot x[n-3] $$

where $h[k]$ are the **filter coefficients** (designed via windowed-sinc or Parks-McClellan) and $x[n-k]$ are the **delayed input samples** (stored in a shift register).

**HDL Constructs (3 marks):**

**(i) 4-Tap FIR Filter (Verilog snippet, simplified):**

```verilog
module fir_filter_4tap (
    input  wire        clk,
    input  wire        reset,
    input  wire signed [7:0]  x_in,
    output reg  signed [15:0] y_out
);
    reg signed [7:0] x_reg [0:3];   // 4-stage shift register
    reg signed [7:0] h [0:3];       // coefficients (loaded from ROM)
    integer i;
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        if (reset) begin
            for (i = 0; i <= 3; i = i + 1) x_reg[i] = 0;
            y_out = 0;
        end else begin
            for (i = 3; i > 0; i = i - 1) x_reg[i] = x_reg[i-1];
            x_reg[0] = x_in;
            y_out = h[0]*x_reg[0] + h[1]*x_reg[1] + h[2]*x_reg[2] + h[3]*x_reg[3];
        end
    end
endmodule
```

**(ii) Coefficient ROM:**

```verilog
module coeff_rom (
    input  wire        clk,
    input  wire [1:0]  addr,
    output reg  signed [7:0] h_out
);
    always @(posedge clk) begin
        case (addr)
            2'd0: h_out = 8'sd10;   // h[0] =  0.0781 * 128
            2'd1: h_out = 8'sd30;   // h[1] =  0.2344 * 128
            2'd2: h_out = 8'sd30;   // h[2] =  0.2344 * 128
            3'd3: h_out = 8'sd10;   // h[3] =  0.0781 * 128
            default: h_out = 0;
        endcase
    end
endmodule
```

**(iii) Testbench for ModelSim:**

```verilog
module tb_fir;
    reg clk, reset;
    reg signed [7:0] x_in;
    wire signed [15:0] y_out;
    fir_filter_4tap uut (.clk(clk), .reset(reset), .x_in(x_in), .y_out(y_out));
    always #5 clk = ~clk;       // 100 MHz clock
    initial begin
        clk = 0; reset = 1; x_in = 0;
        #20 reset = 0;
        x_in = 100; #10;
        x_in = 50;  #10;
        x_in = -30; #10;
        x_in = 80;  #10;
        $finish;
    end
endmodule
```

**Structural Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["x[n] 8-bit Input"] --> B["Shift Register x_reg 0..3"]
    B --> C1["Multiplier h0 * x n"]
    B --> C2["Multiplier h1 * x n-1"]
    B --> C3["Multiplier h2 * x n-2"]
    B --> C4["Multiplier h3 * x n-3"]
    H["Coefficient ROM h 0..3"] --> C1
    H --> C2
    H --> C3
    H --> C4
    C1 --> D["Adder Tree 4-input Sum"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E["Output Register y n 16-bit"]
    E --> F["y_out to DAC or Scope"]
```

**Synthesis Estimate (for Spartan-6 XC6SLX9):**

- **LUTs used:** ~80 (out of 5,720 available) = **1.4% utilization**.
- **DSP48A1 slices used:** 4 (out of 16 available) = **25% utilization** — comfortably within budget.
- **$f_{max}$ achievable:** ~150 MHz (limited by adder tree, not multipliers).

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on the "Project Topics" Question (Internal & External):**
> 
> 1. **Omitting CO/PO mapping (–5 marks):** The single biggest deduction. Every project report **must** include a filled mapping table on page 2. Reports without it are **auto-penalized**.
> 2. **Vague project descriptions (–3 marks):** Saying *"we built a calculator"* loses 2–3 marks. Saying *"we built a 4-bit BCD calculator on Spartan-6 FPGA using VHDL, with 4×4 keypad input, 7-segment display output, and 8 arithmetic operations, mapped to CO1, CO2, CO4"* earns full marks.
> 3. **Skipping simulation evidence (–4 marks):** Hardware-only projects are penalized. Always include **simulation waveforms** in Appendix B.
> 4. **No block diagram (–2 marks):** A clear **block diagram** with labeled signal flow is mandatory in Chapter 3.
> 5. **Missing test case matrix (–3 marks):** A project without a **Pass/Fail table** for at least 10 test cases is considered "unvalidated" by KTU examiners.
> 
> **Bonus Marks (1–2 extra):** Awarded for projects that (a) demonstrate a **real societal impact** (e.g., assistive technology), (b) include a **PCB design** in KiCad, or (c) are published in a **student conference / IEEE regional symposium**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Project Topics:**
> 
> - **Project Bank:** 10 T-Level, 15 D-Level, 15 R-Level topics = **40 total** verified project ideas.
> - **Three Pillars of Selection:** Technical Feasibility (P1), Resource Availability (P2), OBE Compliance (P3).
> - **CO Coverage Threshold:** $\text{Score} = \dfrac{\text{COs addressed}}{5} \geq 0.6$ (i.e., at least 3 of 5 COs).
> - **Mark Distribution:** 50 marks total = 20 internal (Innovation 10 + Implementation 15 wait, this exceeds 20 — recheck) → **20 internal** (Innovation 5, Implementation 7, Presentation 4, Report 4) + **30 external** (viva + demo + report).
> - **Mandatory Report Sections:** Title → Certificate → Abstract → CO/PO Mapping → Introduction → Literature → Design → Implementation → Testing → Conclusion → References → Appendices.
> - **Mandatory Hardware Practice:** Always add **$0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ decoupling capacitor** on every IC's $V_{CC}$ pin.
> - **Mandatory Simulation:** Use TinkerCAD / Logisim / Vivado / ModelSim **before** buying hardware — saves money and time.
> - **7-Phase Lifecycle:** Problem Definition → Literature → Design → Simulation → Hardware → Testing → Report.
> - **Decision Rule:** If $\text{Component Count} > 80$, switch to **microcontroller or FPGA** implementation.
> - **Power Budget Rule:** $PB = V_{CC} \times I_{total} \leq 5\,\text{W}$ for breadboard projects.
> - **FPGA Utilization Target:** LUTs $\leq 50\%$, DSP slices $\leq 75\%$, $f_{max} \geq 50\,\text{MHz}$ for typical PBEET404 projects.
> - **Real-World Mapping:** FPGA projects → VLSI careers; Microcontroller projects → Embedded/IoT careers; Discrete-IC projects → Hardware/PCB careers.
> - **Examiner Pet Peeves:** (1) No CO mapping, (2) No simulation, (3) No block diagram, (4) No test case matrix, (5) Wikipedia references.
> - **One-Line Mantra:** *"Pick a topic you can demonstrate in 10 minutes, document in 25 pages, and explain to a non-engineer in 30 seconds."*
